questions.q: What is the primary purpose of demand forecasting in business decision-making?
options: To eliminate all business risk
To project future values and support informed decisions
To guarantee accurate sales predictions
To replace historical data analysis
To reduce the cost of operations
questions.explain: A forecast is a projection of future values to support informed business decisions. The correct answer directly reflects the definition. Forecasting cannot eliminate risk or guarantee accuracy — it reduces uncertainty by providing systematic projections.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT listed as an element of a good forecast?
options: Accurate
Reliable
Affordable
Meaningful
Effective
questions.explain: The four required attributes are: accurate, reliable, meaningful, and effective. Cost (affordability) is relevant to technique selection but is not listed as a fundamental attribute of the forecast itself. The exam trap is that affordability sounds reasonable but is not part of the core definition.

questions.q: In qualitative forecasting, what is the primary source of information?
options: Historical time-series data
Statistical regression models
Human judgment, opinions, and soft information
Automated sensor networks
Computer algorithms
questions.explain: Qualitative forecasting relies on subjective inputs from people rather than data-based methods. Historical data and algorithms are hallmarks of quantitative forecasting. Qualitative methods are appropriate when data are unavailable or soft factors dominate.

questions.q: Which statement about qualitative vs. quantitative forecasting is correct?
options: Quantitative methods are always superior to qualitative methods
Qualitative methods are inferior because they rely on opinion
Quantitative methods relate dependent variables to causal variables
Qualitative methods use historical time-series patterns
Quantitative methods are never appropriate for new products
questions.explain: Quantitative forecasting relates a dependent variable (e.g. demand) to one or more independent/causal variables (e.g. time, weather, price). The exam trap is equating 'quantitative' with 'better' — qualitative methods are appropriate when data are unavailable or soft factors dominate.

questions.q: The Delphi method was developed by which organization in the 1950s?
options: The World Bank
RAND Corporation
McKinsey & Company
Harvard Business School
The United Nations
questions.explain: The Delphi method was developed by the RAND Corporation in the 1950s. It is an iterative survey process where expert opinions are shared anonymously between rounds until convergence. This is a factual detail commonly tested.

questions.q: What is the defining characteristic of the Delphi method?
options: Survey responses are collected face-to-face
Experts discuss their opinions in a group meeting
Iterative surveys are conducted with anonymity between rounds
Results converge to a single forecast immediately
Salesperson opinions are weighted most heavily
questions.explain: The Delphi method is defined by iterative (repeated) surveys and expert anonymity, which prevents groupthink and dominant personalities from influencing results. Face-to-face meetings and immediate convergence are not part of the method.

questions.q: When should executive opinions be preferred as a forecasting method?
options: For short-term, product-level demand forecasts
For strategic and long-horizon decisions
For new product launches where customer preference is unknown
When historical data are abundant and reliable
When accuracy is the only selection criterion
questions.explain: Executive opinions are best used for strategic and long-horizon decisions because senior managers have broad strategic insight. Salesforce opinions are better for short-term, product-level forecasts. Consumer surveys are appropriate for new product launches.

questions.q: Which forecasting method is most appropriate when no historical data are available?
options: Linear trend equation
Exponential smoothing
Delphi method
Moving average
Naive forecast
questions.explain: The Delphi method is explicitly designed for long-range forecasting when no historical data exist. All other options (trend, smoothing, moving average, naive) require historical time-series data to function.

questions.q: What is a time-series forecast?
options: A forecast that only predicts seasonal variations
A projection that relates demand to economic conditions
A projection of patterns identified in time-ordered observations taken at regular intervals
A method that requires expert opinion
A forecast that eliminates all random variation
questions.explain: A time-series forecast projects patterns from recent time-ordered observations taken at regular intervals, assuming future values can be estimated from past values of the same series. It does not eliminate variation — it accounts for systematic patterns.

questions.q: Which of the following is the core assumption underlying all time-series forecasting methods?
options: Future demand depends only on competitor behavior
Past patterns will repeat in the future
Random variation can be completely eliminated
Human judgment is always required
Trend always increases over time
questions.explain: The core assumption is that future values can be estimated from past values of the same series — i.e., past patterns will repeat or continue. This assumption underlies all time-series techniques.

questions.q: Which of the following is an example of a systematic time-series behavior?
options: A gradual population shift
A labor strike
A natural disaster
An equipment breakdown
An unexpected competitor entry
questions.explain: A gradual population shift is an example of trend — a long-term systematic upward or downward movement. Labor strikes, natural disasters, and equipment breakdowns are examples of irregular variation (identifiable unusual events), not systematic behaviors.

questions.q: What is the distinction between a cycle and seasonality in time-series data?
options: Cycles occur yearly; seasonality occurs monthly
Seasonality is tied to calendar events; cycles last more than one year
Cycles are predictable; seasonality is random
Seasonality is systematic; cycles are always irregular
They are synonymous terms with different names
questions.explain: Seasonality is short-term, fairly regular variation tied to the calendar or time of day. A cycle is a wavelike variation lasting more than one year, often linked to economic, political, or agricultural conditions. This is a common exam confusion point.

questions.q: Which statement correctly distinguishes irregular variation from random variation?
options: They are the same phenomenon with different labels
Irregular variation is caused by identifiable events; random variation is unexplained residual noise
Random variation can be forecast; irregular variation cannot
Irregular variation is random; random variation is predictable
Irregular variation is systematic; random variation is non-systematic
questions.explain: Irregular variation results from identifiable unusual events (strike, natural disaster). Random variation is truly unexplained residual noise that remains after all other behaviors are accounted for. This distinction is an important exam trap.

questions.q: What is the formula for a naive forecast?
options: F_t = a + b × t
F_t = A_t-1
F_t = F_t-1 × seasonal index
questions.explain: The naive forecast formula is F_t = A_t-1; the forecast for period t equals the actual value from the previous period. This is the simplest method and assumes the most recent value is the best predictor of the next value.

questions.q: When is a naive forecast most appropriate to use?
options: Only for data with strong upward trends
When data show seasonal variation or are stable
For forecasts more than five years into the future
When a large amount of historical data is available
When qualitative methods have failed
questions.explain: Naive forecasts are applicable to stable time series, series with seasonal variation (using appropriate adjustment), and even series with trend (with adjustment). They work well when recent past values are good predictors of the future.

questions.q: How does increasing the number of periods (n) in a moving average affect forecast responsiveness?
options: It increases responsiveness to new data
It decreases responsiveness but increases stability
It has no effect on either responsiveness or stability
It improves accuracy but reduces forecast speed
It eliminates the need for weighting
questions.explain: A larger n (more periods) makes the moving average less responsive to recent changes but more stable. A smaller n makes it more responsive to new data but less stable. This inverse relationship is key to understanding parameter selection.

questions.q: A moving average forecast uses the average of the five most recent actual values. Which statement is true?
options: All historical values remain in the calculation
The oldest value is dropped and the newest value is added
As new data arrive, both the oldest and newest values are dropped
Only the newest value affects the next forecast
The weights are uniform across all historical data
questions.explain: In a moving average, as new data arrive, the newest value is added and the oldest is dropped; the average is recomputed with only the n most recent values. This rolling window gives equal weight to each value within it.

questions.q: What is the primary advantage of weighted moving average over simple moving average?
options: It requires fewer historical data points
It automatically selects the best weights
It allows more recent periods to receive higher weight
It eliminates the need to choose a value for n
It guarantees perfect accuracy
questions.explain: Weighted moving average assigns different weights to each past period, typically giving higher weight to recent data. This provides more control than simple moving average, though weight selection requires trial and error.

questions.q: In a weighted moving average, what constraint must apply to the weights?
options: They must sum to 1
They must all be equal
They must be positive integers
They must decrease monotonically
No constraint — weights can be any number
questions.explain: The weights in a weighted moving average must sum to 1 (or 100%). This ensures that the result is a true weighted average, not an arbitrary linear combination.

questions.q: Exponential smoothing can be understood as a special case of which other method?
options: Naive forecasting
Linear trend equation
Weighted moving average
Delphi method
Seasonal adjustment
questions.explain: Exponential smoothing is a special case of weighted moving average in which weights decline exponentially for older periods. This relationship is an important exam trap — students often treat it as a completely separate method.

questions.q: What does a high smoothing constant (α) in exponential smoothing imply?
options: More weight on older data
More weight on the most recent observation
Less responsive to recent changes
Lower forecast accuracy
Greater stability in the forecast
questions.explain: Higher α (e.g. 0.9) means more weight on the most recent observation A_t-1, making the forecast more responsive. Lower α (e.g. 0.1) gives more weight to the previous forecast, yielding a smoother, more stable forecast. This direction is commonly confused on exams.

questions.q: In the exponential smoothing formula F_t = F_t-1 + α(A_t-1 − F_t-1), what does the term (A_t-1 − F_t-1) represent?
options: The seasonal adjustment
The error from the previous period
The forecast for the next period
The trend slope
The weighting adjustment factor
questions.explain: The term (A_t-1 − F_t-1) is the error from the previous period — the difference between actual and forecast. The parameter α (smoothing constant) determines what percentage of this error is fed back into the next forecast.

questions.q: What does the linear trend equation F_t = a + b×t measure?
options: Seasonal patterns in the data
A consistent upward or downward movement in the data over time
Random variation around the trend
The moving average of recent values
Cyclical business behavior
questions.explain: The linear trend equation models a consistent upward or downward movement (trend) over time. The slope b represents the rate of change, and the intercept a is the forecast value at t=0.

questions.q: In a linear trend equation F_t = a + b×t, what does the slope (b) represent?
options: The number of observations used
The rate of change per time period
The average value of the series
The starting forecast value
The seasonal adjustment factor
questions.explain: The slope b is the rate of change per time period — it tells you how much the forecast changes for each additional period. A positive slope indicates upward trend; negative indicates downward trend.

questions.q: Which model is appropriate when seasonal variations grow larger as the series level increases?
options: Naive forecast with adjustment
Linear trend model
Multiplicative seasonality model
Additive seasonality model
Exponential smoothing without adjustment
questions.explain: In the multiplicative model, the seasonal effect is expressed as a percentage of the average/trend, then multiplied into the series value. As the level increases, the seasonal swing grows proportionally. In the additive model, the seasonal component has fixed absolute magnitude.

questions.q: What is the key difference between additive and multiplicative seasonality models?
options: Additive models work with trends; multiplicative models do not
Additive: fixed quantity added/subtracted; Multiplicative: percentage multiplied by series level
They are mathematically equivalent but differently named
Multiplicative models are less accurate than additive
Additive models are for long-term forecasts only
questions.explain: Additive seasonality expresses the seasonal effect as a fixed quantity (absolute amount). Multiplicative seasonality expresses it as a percentage of the average/trend, then multiplies into the series. This difference is a frequent exam trap.

questions.q: What is forecast error defined as in this course?
options: Actual minus Forecast
The difference between two forecasts
Forecast minus Actual
The variance of the forecast distribution
The percentage change from one period to the next
questions.explain: Forecast error is defined as e_t = A_t − F_t (Actual minus Forecast). This is the standard definition in forecasting. Positive errors mean actual exceeded forecast; negative errors mean actual fell short.

questions.q: Which accuracy metric penalizes large forecast errors more heavily than small ones?
options: MSE (Mean Squared Error)
MAD (Mean Absolute Deviation)
MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error)
All three metrics weight errors equally
None of the metrics differentiate error sizes
questions.explain: MSE (Mean Squared Error) penalizes large errors more heavily because the errors are squared before averaging. MAD weights all errors equally; MAPE weights errors by relative size. This difference is critical for selecting the appropriate metric.

questions.q: When should MAPE be preferred over MAD or MSE for measuring forecast accuracy?
options: When all series have similar average values
When comparing accuracy across series with different scales or units
When the forecast horizon exceeds one year
When human judgment is the primary data source
When demand is seasonal
questions.explain: MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error) expresses error as a percentage, making it useful for comparing accuracy across series with different absolute scales (e.g. comparing a product with 1,000 units sold to one with 100,000 units sold).

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a factor in selecting a forecasting technique?
options: Cost of implementation and operation
The color of the data visualization
Availability of historical data
Accuracy (measured by MAD, MSE, MAPE)
Forecast horizon (short-term vs. long-term)
questions.explain: The six legitimate selection factors are: cost, accuracy, historical data availability, software availability, time to prepare, and forecast horizon. The color of visualization is irrelevant to technique selection. This is a straightforward identification question.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT one of the seven core concepts of revenue management?
options: Focus on price rather than costs
Replace cost-based pricing with market-based pricing
Maximize market share through volume discounts
Save products for most valuable customers
Continually reevaluate revenue opportunities
questions.explain: The seven core concepts focus on pricing strategy, customer segmentation, data-driven decisions, and value cycles. Maximizing market share through volume discounts contradicts concept #4 (saving capacity for high-value customers). This is an exam trap because volume growth may seem aligned with revenue thinking, but it sacrifices margin.

questions.q: What is the primary advantage of market-based pricing over cost-plus pricing?
options: Lower administrative costs
Pricing flexibility not constrained by cost structure
Easier to implement across all customer segments
Reduced competition from rivals
Automatic quality improvements
questions.explain: Market-based pricing is anchored to customer willingness to pay, which can adjust regardless of cost changes. Cost-plus pricing is rigid: if costs stay flat but demand spikes, the firm leaves money on the table. Market-based pricing leverages demand uncertainty as an advantage rather than hiding behind costs.

questions.q: In the newsvendor booking limit model, what does the underage cost represent?
options: The variable cost per low-fare ticket sold
The loss from turning away high-fare customers when protecting too few seats
The revenue forgone by leaving a seat empty
The administrative cost of managing booking blocks
The discount rate applied by discount-tier customers
questions.explain: Underage cost = p_H − p_L, not p_H. It is the fare difference lost when a high-fare customer cannot be served because the seat was sold to a low-fare buyer. This is an exam trap because students often think the underage cost is the full high fare, forgetting that the alternative use (low fare) still generates revenue.

questions.q: When is the booking limit decision framework most relevant?
options: When a firm has excess capacity and can serve all customers
When demand for all segments is deterministic and known
When total demand likely exceeds capacity and the same resource serves multiple segments
When customers have identical willingness to pay
When legal price discrimination is prohibited
questions.explain: Booking limits ration limited capacity across high- and low-value segments. With excess capacity, there is no need to protect seats — all customers are served at their segment price. Booking limits only add value when demand is uncertain and likely to exceed capacity.

questions.q: What is the critical ratio (CR) formula?
options: (p_H − p_L) / p_L
(p_H − p_L) / p_H
p_H / (p_H + p_L)
questions.explain: CR = (p_H − p_L) / p_H represents the service level for the high-fare class. This ratio determines how much of the demand distribution to include in the optimal reservation quantity. When CR = 0.50, optimal Q = mean demand; when CR > 0.50, the mean is adjusted upward by a multiple of the standard deviation.

questions.q: How does the nested booking structure protect revenue for high-fare customers?
options: By offering high-fare customers a separate aircraft
By ensuring a protected quantity of seats is reserved and not released to low-fare sales
By charging lower prices early and higher prices closer to departure
By limiting the total number of bookings on any flight
By segregating customers based on their purchase history
questions.explain: Nested control reserves N seats for full-fare buyers; this protected quantity is not offered to discount-fare or medium-fare buyers. Lower-fare sales only consume from the unprotected pool. As departure approaches, protections are released progressively to fill remaining empty seats.

questions.q: A firm uses single-price selling at $50 on a demand curve ranging from $99 to $1 (linear). Approximately how much additional revenue is captured by switching to four equal-priced tiers ($20, $40, $60, $80)?
options: 10% increase
40% increase
60% increase
100% increase
200% increase
questions.explain: Single price $50 → 50 units × $50 = $2,500. Four-tier pricing → 20 units at each of $20, $40, $60, $80 = $400 + $800 + $1,200 + $1,600 = $4,000. The increase is (4000 − 2500) / 2500 = 60%. This is a direct application of the lecture example and demonstrates the revenue loss from ignoring willingness-to-pay segmentation.

questions.q: What problem does consumer surplus represent in the single-price revenue model?
options: The total cost of production for all units sold
The value that customers capture when willing to pay more than the offered price
The number of unsatisfied customers who cannot afford the product
The difference between variable cost and selling price
The profit margin on each unit sold at the optimal price
questions.explain: Consumer surplus = Willingness to Pay − Offered Price. At a single price of $50, customers willing to pay $99 capture surplus of $49 each. Price segmentation recovers this surplus for the firm. This is not a cost; it is captured value being left with customers.

questions.q: Under what conditions are booking limits appropriate?
options: The firm produces custom products for each customer
Same capacity serves multiple segments with different valuations, demand exceeds capacity, and price discrimination is legal
All customers must be served at identical prices
Demand is perfectly predictable and deterministic
The product has infinite supply
questions.explain: Booking limits require: shared capacity across segments, different segment valuations, legal price discrimination fences, and demand uncertainty with excess demand. All four conditions must hold. If any is missing, booking limits add no value.

questions.q: Which of the following best illustrates the shift from cost-centric to market-centric thinking in revenue management?
options: Reducing production costs by 10%
Charging what the market bears rather than basing price on cost plus a fixed markup
Increasing product volume sold annually
Improving manufacturing efficiency
Lowering the break-even quantity
questions.explain: Revenue management's core concept #2 explicitly states: replace cost-based pricing (cost + markup) with market-based pricing (what customers will pay). The shift recognizes that pricing has more flexibility than cost reduction and exploits demand uncertainty as an advantage.

questions.q: If the critical ratio CR = 0.667 for a booking limit problem, how does this affect the optimal reservation quantity compared to the case when CR = 0.50?
options: Optimal Q decreases below the mean
Optimal Q stays at the mean (no adjustment for CR)
Optimal Q increases above the mean by a multiple of the standard deviation
Optimal Q is set to the maximum possible capacity
Optimal Q becomes zero (protect no seats for high-fare)
questions.explain: When CR = 0.50, optimal Q = mean. When CR = 0.667 > 0.50, underage cost exceeds overage cost, so the firm protects more seats; optimal Q = mean + NORMSINV(0.667) × stdev ≈ mean + 0.431 × stdev. Higher CR incentivizes higher protection.

questions.q: What is the overage cost in the newsvendor booking limit model?
options: The full high-fare price p_H
The difference p_H − p_L
The low-fare price p_L
The variable cost of the service
The cost of blocking a seat for discount fares
questions.explain: Overage cost = p_L because if the firm protects more seats than demanded for high-fare, those unoccupied seats are forgone sales at the low-fare price. Every empty seat is lost low-fare revenue. Underage cost is p_H − p_L because a turned-away high-fare customer loses only the fare difference from what the low-fare customer would pay.

questions.q: Which pricing method is explicitly designed for real-time capacity and demand adjustments?
options: Cost-plus pricing
Willingness-to-pay pricing
Dynamic pricing
Fixed segment pricing
Historical average pricing
questions.explain: Dynamic pricing adjusts price in real time based on observed inventory, evolving demand, and remaining time. This is the hallmark of modern revenue management (airlines, ride-share, hotels) and contrasts with static approaches like cost-plus or segment-fixed pricing.

questions.q: In the nested booking control system, what happens ~2 weeks before departure?
options: All remaining inventory is sold at deep discounts
Discount fares are blocked from booking, followed later by medium fares
Full-fare seats are released to all customer classes
The entire pricing structure is reset to a single price
All bookings are cancelled and refunded
questions.explain: The timeline is: open booking → ~2 weeks before, block discount fares (stop selling them) → then block medium fares → departure. This progressively releases protected seats as the departure date approaches. By the time the flight departs, only booked seats remain.

questions.q: How does price discrimination via segmentation increase firm revenue in the lecture's $1–$99 demand example?
options: By lowering costs on all units
By capturing consumer surplus that was previously left with customers or lost to rejected demand
By reducing the quantity demanded
By forcing customers to pay higher prices on average
By eliminating the need for customer market research
questions.explain: The single-price firm leaves consumer surplus with high-willingness-to-pay customers and rejects price-sensitive customers entirely. Segmentation captures surplus from the first group and serves the second group at a lower price. Both effects increase total revenue, moving it from $2,500 to $4,000.

questions.q: What does the term 'cannibalization' in the context of multi-product pricing refer to?
options: Destruction of a competitor's market position
Intentional price wars to drive competitors out
Lower-tier customers switching to cheaper products or lower-priced segments to avoid paying premium prices
The physical degradation of product quality over time
A decrease in total market size
questions.explain: Cannibalization occurs when offering a low-priced tier causes customers to shift from a higher-priced tier. For example, if a firm offers both a Professional and a Basic version, some Large Corporations might buy Basic instead of Professional, reducing overall revenue. This is why multi-product optimization must model self-selection behavior.

questions.q: Which core concept of revenue management directly contradicts the goal of maximizing volume sales through low prices?
options: Exploit value cycles
Save products for most valuable customers
Continually reevaluate revenue opportunities
Use data-driven decisions
Replace cost-based pricing
questions.explain: Concept #4 explicitly states to 'save products for most valuable customers,' which means rationing capacity to high-value segments rather than flooding the market with low-price sales. This is fundamentally opposed to pure volume maximization.

questions.q: In a booking limit decision, if actual high-fare demand D exceeds the protected quantity Q, what is the consequence?
options: Extra revenue is earned by selling more at high fares
High-fare customers are turned away and the firm forgoes fare-difference revenue
The firm must immediately increase the price of low-fare tickets
Low-fare inventory is automatically released to high-fare
No consequence; demand can always be satisfied
questions.explain: If D > Q (underage), the firm has protected too few seats and cannot serve all high-fare demand. The cost is p_H − p_L per turned-away customer, not the full p_H, because those seats could have been sold at the low fare. This is why the critical ratio balances underage and overage costs.

questions.q: What is the relationship between market-based pricing and demand uncertainty in revenue management?
options: Demand uncertainty makes pricing impossible
Market-based pricing is indifferent to demand conditions
Demand uncertainty is the central challenge that makes market-based pricing and segmentation valuable
Demand certainty enables market-based pricing
Market-based pricing eliminates demand uncertainty
questions.explain: The lecture states that revenue management 'uses demand uncertainty as the central challenge,' not a liability. Uncertainty justifies maintaining flexible pricing and reserving capacity for high-value demand, which may materialize late in the booking horizon.

questions.q: Why might willingness-to-pay pricing require 'dangerous' price discrimination fences?
options: To prevent customer confusion about pricing
To avoid legal challenges to price discrimination and prevent customers from arbitraging between price tiers
To ensure low-income customers are excluded
To increase administrative complexity
To reduce competition from rival firms
questions.explain: Price discrimination is 'dangerous' because customers may find ways to circumvent price tiers (e.g., by falsely claiming student status). Fences like advance purchase, refundability, or product version differentiation legally separate customer classes and prevent arbitrage that would collapse the segmentation.

questions.q: Under what circumstance is the overage cost in the newsvendor booking limit model equal to zero?
options: When p_H equals p_L
When the low-fare price is zero or the empty seat cannot be sold to any customer
When all customers are willing to pay the high fare
When the firm has unlimited capacity
When demand is perfectly predictable
questions.explain: Overage cost = p_L. If p_L = 0 (no salvage value for unsold seats), then overage cost is zero. In airlines, empty seats cannot be sold at any price once the flight departs, making p_L effectively zero. This would shift the optimal quantity toward higher protection.

questions.q: How does the seven-concept framework reframe the firm's strategic focus in revenue management?
options: From sales volume to profit maximization
From production efficiency to market-driven pricing and segmentation
From customer satisfaction to pure cost reduction
From innovation to maintaining status quo
From global expansion to domestic markets only
questions.explain: The lecture states that revenue management 'shifts the firm's focus from cost-based thinking to market-based thinking.' The seven concepts emphasize pricing flexibility, customer segmentation, data-driven decisions, and value cycles — all rooted in market dynamics rather than internal cost structures.

questions.q: What is the primary risk of ignoring willingness-to-pay segmentation?
options: Prices become too low for all customers
Revenue is left on the table through consumer surplus and rejected demand
Costs increase due to complex pricing systems
Customers stop purchasing entirely
Competitors will copy the pricing structure
questions.explain: The lecture example shows that single-price selling at $50 generates $2,500 revenue while ignoring 49 customers willing to pay > $50 (consumer surplus) and rejecting 49 customers willing to pay < $50. Segmentation recovers $4,000, a 60% increase. The risk is thus lost revenue, not cost inflation.

questions.q: In multi-product revenue management, why might Large Corporations and Laboratories be considered 'natural targets' for premium products?
options: They have the largest number of employees
They have the lowest willingness to pay for any product
They have very high valuations for Professional and Advanced products relative to other segments
They reject all discount products
They offer the lowest profit margins
questions.explain: The valuation table shows Large Corporations willing to pay $1,200 (Pro) and $2,500 (Advanced), and Laboratories willing to pay $1,000 (Pro) and $2,000 (Advanced). These are disproportionately high compared to other segments, making premium products the natural revenue driver for these segments.

questions.q: What adjustment to the optimal booking quantity is needed when the critical ratio CR deviates from 0.50?
options: The quantity decreases proportionally to CR
The quantity remains fixed at the mean regardless of CR
The quantity is adjusted by adding or subtracting a multiple of the standard deviation using NORMSINV(CR)
The quantity is set to the maximum capacity
The quantity is set to zero
questions.explain: When CR = 0.50, costs are symmetric and optimal Q = mean. When CR ≠ 0.50, asymmetric costs justify a mean adjustment: optimal Q = mean + NORMSINV(CR) × stdev. For example, CR = 2/3 ≈ 0.667 gives NORMSINV(2/3) ≈ 0.431, so Q = mean + 0.431 × stdev.

questions.q: What does the 'booking limit for the low-fare class' equal in nested booking control?
options: The high-fare price divided by the low-fare price
Available Capacity minus the Reserved Quantity for the high-fare class
The total demand minus low-fare demand
The number of seats available after departure
The average of high-fare and low-fare prices
questions.explain: The lecture states: 'Booking Limit for low-fare class = Available Capacity − Reserved Quantity for high-fare class.' This formula ensures that high-fare reservations are protected; any remaining capacity after reservations can be offered to low-fare customers.

questions.q: When students receive a 40% bookstore commission on sales, how does this affect the effective revenue per unit for the student segment?
options: Effective revenue becomes 40% of list price
Effective revenue remains 100% of list price
Effective revenue becomes 60% of list price
Effective revenue increases to 140% of list price
Effective revenue becomes 160% of list price
questions.explain: If students must pay a 40% commission to the bookstore, the firm retains only 60% of the list price as effective revenue. This 60% multiplier must be applied when calculating profit margins and pricing optimization for the student segment.

questions.q: Which of the following scenarios would most clearly violate the conditions for applying booking limits?
options: An airline with multiple fare classes and uncertain demand
A hotel with high-season and low-season room rates
A software company with unlimited license capacity and no demand uncertainty
A concert with VIP and standard seating tiers
A rental car company with early-bird and last-minute rates
questions.explain: Booking limits require: same capacity for multiple segments, demand uncertainty, and total demand likely exceeding capacity. Unlimited software licenses eliminate the scarcity condition (no capacity constraint), and deterministic demand eliminates uncertainty. Without these, booking limits add no value.

questions.q: What is the key insight about protecting seats in the booking limit formula when CR = 0.50 versus CR = 2/3?
options: When CR = 0.50, protect more seats than when CR = 2/3
When CR = 0.50, protect fewer seats than when CR = 2/3
Both CR values lead to identical protection levels
CR has no effect on the protection quantity
Protection decreases linearly as CR increases
questions.explain: CR = 0.50 yields symmetric costs; optimal Q = mean only. CR = 2/3 yields asymmetric costs favoring protection; optimal Q = mean + 0.431 × stdev. Higher CR (stronger underprotection cost) justifies protecting more seats.

questions.q: How do the booking limits and dynamic pricing strategies differ in their approach to capacity allocation?
options: Booking limits and dynamic pricing are identical approaches
Booking limits fix a protection quantity in advance; dynamic pricing continuously adjusts prices based on real-time conditions
Booking limits allow continuous repricing; dynamic pricing sets prices once
Booking limits are only for airlines; dynamic pricing is only for hotels
Both strategies ignore demand uncertainty
questions.explain: Booking limits decide a protected quantity Q before demand is realized (newsvendor-style), using the critical ratio. Dynamic pricing continuously adjusts price based on observed inventory and demand signals. Booking limits are discrete (protect N or don't); dynamic pricing is continuous.

lectures.title: Lecture 9: Revenue Management and Pricing Part I

questions.q: Which of the following best describes the primary focus shift in revenue management?
options: From market-based pricing to cost-plus pricing
From cost focus to market-based price focus
From segmentation to mass market targeting
From price discrimination to uniform pricing
From data-driven decisions to intuition-based strategies
questions.explain: Revenue management's core concept 1 explicitly shifts focus from costs to price as the lever for balancing supply and demand. Cost-plus pricing is what it replaces, not what it adopts.

questions.q: What is meant by 'micro-market segmentation' in the context of revenue management?
options: Selling identical products at identical prices to all customers
Selling to multiple small segments with different willingness-to-pay rather than one undifferentiated mass market
Dividing costs equally across market regions
Reducing the number of customer groups to simplify operations
Using geographic location as the only segmentation variable
questions.explain: Micro-market segmentation (Core Concept 3) explicitly targets multiple smaller segments with tailored pricing, the opposite of undifferentiated mass market sales. Geography alone is too coarse.

questions.q: In the booking limit framework, what are the two primary functions of booking limits?
options: Reduce costs and increase inventory
Lock in demand early and enable price discrimination across segments
Eliminate no-shows and prevent overbooking
Standardize pricing and reduce customer confusion
Maximize occupancy of low-value seats only
questions.explain: Booking limits secure early low-fare demand and permit different prices for the same capacity across customer segments, directly enabling revenue management's goal of capturing surplus.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a necessary condition for deploying booking limits?
options: Same capacity serves multiple customer segments
Segments have different willingness to pay
Firm can legally price discriminate
Total demand is certain and equals capacity exactly
Demand is uncertain and likely exceeds capacity
questions.explain: Booking limits require uncertain demand that exceeds capacity; if demand equals capacity exactly, there is no rationing decision to make. All other conditions are required.

questions.q: What does 'nested control' mean in the context of fare classes?
options: Lower fare classes can access seats reserved for higher fare classes
Higher fare classes can always access any remaining seats at lower fare prices
Each fare class gets a separate, non-overlapping bucket of seats
Fare classes are determined by nested algorithms only
Control is transferred from airlines to customers
questions.explain: Nested control means the capacity hierarchy is stacked such that full-fare passengers are never blocked — they can use any remaining seat, regardless of which class it was initially allocated to.

questions.q: In the newsvendor booking-limit framework, what does the 'underage cost' represent?
options: The cost of overselling capacity beyond physical limits
The revenue lost when not enough high-fare customers materialize, forcing sales at low fare instead
The cost of holding excess inventory unsold
The administrative cost of managing multiple fare classes
The penalty charged by customers for long wait times
questions.explain: Underage cost (c_u) = p_H − p_L. It is the margin lost when a high-fare customer could have paid p_H but instead sells at p_L due to under-protection of capacity.

questions.q: In the newsvendor booking-limit model, the overage cost is equal to which value?
options: p_H (the high fare price)
p_H − p_L (the margin)
p_L (the low fare price)
p_H + p_L (the sum)
p_L − p_H (the negative difference)
questions.explain: Overage cost (c_o) = p_L because over-protecting leaves a seat empty that could have sold to a low-fare customer. The exam trap warns that c_o is NOT p_H.

questions.q: Given the formula Critical Ratio = (p_H − p_L) / p_H, what does this ratio represent?
options: The percentage of seats to allocate to discount fares
The threshold quantile of the demand distribution for optimal protection
The profit margin for high-fare passengers
The percentage increase in revenue from price discrimination
The ratio of high-fare to low-fare customers
questions.explain: The critical ratio is the probability threshold used with NORMINV to find the optimal protection level Q. It balances overage and underage costs.

questions.q: When the critical ratio equals 0.50, what is NORMSINV(0.5)?
options: Undefined
questions.explain: NORMSINV(0.5) = 0 because 0.5 is the median of the standard normal distribution. Thus Q = μ_H + 0×σ_H = μ_H (the mean demand).

questions.q: In Example 1 (p_H = $1,000, p_L = $500), what is the critical ratio?
questions.explain: CR = ($1,000 − $500) / $1,000 = $500 / $1,000 = 0.50. Underage and overage costs are equal, so the critical ratio is exactly 0.5.

questions.q: In Example 2 (p_H = $1,500, p_L = $500), what is the critical ratio?
questions.explain: CR = ($1,500 − $500) / $1,500 = $1,000 / $1,500 = 2/3 ≈ 0.667. The higher high-fare price increases the underage penalty relative to overage.

questions.q: In Example 2, how does the optimal booking protection Q change compared to Example 1?
options: Q remains equal to μ_H with no z-score adjustment
Q increases by adding a positive z-score multiple to μ_H
Q decreases by subtracting from μ_H
Q becomes independent of the demand distribution
Q equals the booking limit for low-fare passengers
questions.explain: In Example 2, CR = 2/3 > 0.5, so NORMSINV(2/3) > 0. Thus Q = μ_H + (positive value)×σ_H, protecting more capacity than the mean demand.

questions.q: What is consumer surplus in the context of price and willingness to pay?
options: The total revenue earned by the firm
The difference between what a customer is willing to pay and the actual price paid
The cost savings achieved by bulk purchasing
The remaining inventory after all sales
The profit margin on each unit sold
questions.explain: Consumer surplus is WTP minus the offered price. It represents value that the customer retains; revenue management aims to capture this surplus by raising prices closer to WTP.

questions.q: In the single-price demand scenario (linear, 1 unit at $99, 99 units at $1), what is the profit-maximizing single price?
questions.explain: With linear demand, the profit-maximizing price occurs at the midpoint: $50. At this price, 50 units sell, generating revenue of $2,500.

questions.q: How much revenue is lost due to consumer surplus and rejected customers when using a single $50 price in the demand example?
questions.explain: At price $50, 50 units sell for $2,500 revenue. The remaining 49 customers have WTP < $50, so they are rejected. A perfectly discriminating firm could capture all 99 units.

questions.q: When offering four price levels ($20, $40, $60, $80) in the demand example, approximately what is the total revenue?
questions.explain: Selling 20 units at each of $20, $40, $60, and $80 gives 20×(20+40+60+80) = 20×200 = $4,000. This is a 60% increase over the single-price revenue.

questions.q: What percentage increase in revenue occurs when moving from single-price to four-tier pricing in the demand example?
What is the main risk when offering multiple price tiers openly to customers?
options: Customers are confused by too many options
Segments may cross over to lower-priced tiers, requiring fences or restrictions to prevent cannibalization
Inventory runs out too quickly
Administrative costs increase exponentially
Regulatory agencies prohibit price discrimination
questions.explain: The notes explicitly state that offering multiple prices openly is 'dangerous' because customers may shift to cheaper tiers. Firms must use fences (e.g., advance purchase, restrictions) to maintain segmentation.

questions.q: In multi-product revenue management, what does 'segment valuation' refer to?
options: The cost to develop a new product
The maximum price each market segment is willing to pay for each product version
The number of units each segment will purchase
The total market size in dollars
The profit margin on each product
questions.explain: Segment valuations are the willingness-to-pay amounts for each product tier (Basic/Professional/Advanced) within each segment (Consultants/Small Business/etc.).

questions.q: According to the multi-product example, which segment shows the highest willingness-to-pay for the Advanced product?
options: Consultants ($600)
Small Businesses ($300)
Large Corporations ($2,500)
Laboratories ($2,000)
Students ($100)
questions.explain: Large Corporations have the highest Advanced WTP at $2,500, followed by Laboratories at $2,000. This segment is a premium target for Advanced pricing.

questions.q: In the multi-product example, what unique adjustment is required for the student segment pricing?
options: Students receive a 50% discount on all products
Bookstores take a 40% commission, so the firm nets only 60% of student prices
Students must pay upfront before delivery
Students are excluded from the Advanced product tier
The firm charges students a higher development cost
questions.explain: The example explicitly notes that bookstores take 40% commission on student sales, meaning the firm's net revenue is only 60% of the listed student price — a key adjustment in profitability calculations.

questions.q: What is the development cost for the Professional product in the multi-product example?
questions.explain: According to the Product Options table, Professional has a development cost of $200,000 and a variable cost of $25 per unit.

questions.q: Which dynamic pricing method is based on the assumption that price responds to observed inventory and demand conditions?
options: Cost-plus pricing
Booking limits
Dynamic pricing
Willingness-to-pay pricing
Overbooking strategy
questions.explain: Dynamic pricing explicitly adjusts price in response to observed or estimated inventory levels, demand, and time — the core operational tool of revenue management.

questions.q: How does the 'exploit the value cycle' principle apply to revenue management?
options: Products should maintain constant prices throughout their lifecycle
Prices should be adjusted as a product's perceived value changes over time
Costs decrease over time, so prices should also decrease
Only new products are subject to pricing adjustments
The value cycle is irrelevant to pricing decisions
questions.explain: Core Concept 6 states that each product's perceived value changes over time; firms should capitalize on these shifts by adjusting prices dynamically.

questions.q: What does 'continual re-evaluation' in the seven core concepts mean?
options: Prices should never change once set
Revenue opportunities are fixed and predictable
Firms must regularly reassess revenue opportunities as conditions shift
Only executives are responsible for decisions
Re-evaluation occurs only annually
questions.explain: Core Concept 7 emphasizes that revenue opportunities are dynamic; firms must reassess regularly to stay aligned with market conditions.

questions.q: In the context of overbooking, what is the underlying assumption?
options: The firm intentionally oversells to increase revenue regardless of no-shows
The firm accepts more reservations than capacity, anticipating some customers will not show up
Overbooking is illegal in all industries
It applies only to hotels, not airlines
It eliminates the need for booking limits
questions.explain: Overbooking is a risk-management strategy where firms accept more reservations than physical capacity, betting that some percentage of customers will be no-shows.

questions.q: Which industries commonly use booking limits according to the lecture?
options: Manufacturing and wholesaling only
Airlines, hotels, car rentals, movie theaters, concerts, and events
Only financial services and insurance
Retail stores exclusively
Government agencies only
questions.explain: The lecture explicitly lists airlines, hotels, car rentals, movie theaters, concerts, and events as common users of booking limits.

questions.q: When blocking discount fares in nested control, when does this typically occur?
options: Immediately after the booking window opens
Earliest in the booking window, well before departure
Only on the day of departure
After medium fares are fully booked
Never — discount fares are always available
questions.explain: In nested control, discount fares are blocked first because the firm wants to secure high-value full-fare customers closer to departure. The booking window in this model opens ~2 weeks before departure.

questions.q: What is the relationship between the booking limit for low-fare passengers and the optimal protection level Q?
options: They are the same value
Booking Limit (L) = Total Capacity − Q
Q = Booking Limit (L) + 1
Booking Limit (L) = Q × 2
There is no fixed relationship
questions.explain: Once Q (optimal protection for high-fare) is determined via the critical ratio and NORMINV, the booking limit for low-fare passengers is simply: Total Capacity − Q.

questions.q: How many of the five market segments in the multi-product example have a development cost of at least $200,000?
options: One
Two
Three
Four
All five
questions.explain: Consultants ($200,000), Small Businesses ($200,000), and Students ($300,000) all have development costs ≥ $200,000. That is three segments.

lectures.title: Lecture 10: What is Common Between Zara and 7-Eleven: Supply Chain Management Best Practices

questions.q: What is Zara's market positioning in the fashion industry?
options: Luxury designer brand with premium pricing
Cheap chic — fashion quality like Banana Republic with Old Navy prices
High-end fast fashion competing on design originality
Budget brand with basic item focus and minimal variety
Value retailer sourcing entirely from low-cost Asian manufacturers
questions.explain: Zara explicitly occupies the 'cheap chic' (廉價時尚) quadrant, combining mid-tier fashion quality with accessible pricing. Students often confuse Zara with pure luxury brands; the key insight is that Zara is a trend follower and imitator, not a high-end originator.

questions.q: How much does Zara spend on marketing as a percentage of revenue compared to the industry average?
questions.explain: Zara's marketing spend is only 0.3% of revenue versus the industry average of ~3.5%. This stark difference reveals Zara's strategy: rely on store location and rapid product turnover ('buy now or never' urgency) instead of expensive advertising campaigns.

questions.q: Which of the following best explains why Zara manufactures high-fashion items in Spain, Portugal, and Morocco despite much higher labor costs than Asia?
options: To maintain European brand prestige and luxury positioning
To reduce distribution and logistics costs within Europe
To achieve fast lead times and responsiveness — speed is prioritized over cost savings
To comply with European Union trade regulations
To support local communities and fulfill corporate social responsibility
questions.explain: Proximity manufacturing is chosen explicitly for speed and responsiveness, NOT cost savings — Spanish labor costs (~$1,650/month) far exceed Guangdong minimums (~$289/month). This illustrates that operational design choices about lead time directly determine competitive position. Students often incorrectly assume cost is the primary driver.

questions.q: What is the primary purpose of Zara's small batch size strategy?
options: To reduce manufacturing waste and defects
To create artificial scarcity and encourage immediate purchase ('buy now or never')
To test new designs with minimal financial risk
To optimize warehouse storage efficiency
To improve inventory turnover across all SKUs equally
questions.explain: Small batches deliberately create artificial scarcity (人工稀缺感), triggering consumer psychology: if the item is not in stock now, it may never return. This drives the remarkably high visitation rate (17 times/year vs. 3 industry average). While it also limits downside on failures, the primary mechanism is psychological urgency.

questions.q: Zara achieves design-to-store lead times as fast as 1 week through which combination of operational choices?
options: Exclusively outsourcing manufacturing to pre-positioned supplier networks in low-cost countries
Maintaining internal design teams with cross-functional collaboration, proximity manufacturing, and rapid decision approval
Using pure make-to-order production to minimize inventory risk
Relying entirely on advanced demand forecasting to pre-produce inventory
Centralizing all decision-making authority at global headquarters
questions.explain: Cross-functional teams (spanning design, merchandising, sourcing, and distribution) can examine prototypes, choose a design, and commit resources in hours. Proximity manufacturing enables 24-hour European delivery. Decentralized store-level decisions keep momentum high. No single element alone achieves this speed.

questions.q: In Zara's closed-loop information flow, which role acts as the bridge between customer demand signals and production commitments?
options: Fashion Buyer — translates store signals into design and sourcing decisions
Store Manager — directly captures customer feedback
Designer — interprets buyer requests into producible prototypes
Production Manager — schedules manufacturing based on designer specifications
Warehouse Manager — determines final assortment shipped to stores
questions.explain: The information flow is: Customer → Store Manager → Buyer → Designer → Production Manager. The Fashion Buyer's role is critical as the translator: they receive POS data and soft data from stores, then direct the designer and production manager. Store managers feed raw demand signals; the buyer turns them into actionable decisions.

questions.q: What does Zara's use of undyed fabrics and colors adapted mid-season illustrate about the postponement strategy?
options: Postponement means delaying all production until customer orders arrive
Postponement keeps inputs uncommitted so final configuration happens after demand clarity — a portion of inventory remains flexible
Postponement requires smaller total inventory compared to traditional forecasting
Postponement eliminates the need for demand forecasting entirely
Postponement applies only to commodity products with stable demand
questions.explain: Over 50% of Zara's fabrics are kept undyed, allowing color choices to be made mid-season in response to actual demand signals. This is NOT complete production delay — it is strategic inventory flexibility. The exam trap is that students think postponement means inaction; it actually means phasing commitment strategically. Benetton pioneered this concept.

questions.q: How does Zara's 7-season production model differ fundamentally from the traditional 4-season model?
options: Zara introduces designs twice as frequently as traditional retailers
Zara overlaps design and raw material sourcing phases, allowing heavier late-stage production commitment based on actual demand signals
Zara extends lead times to allow for more thorough quality control
Zara divides the year into seven equal inventory replenishment cycles
Zara manufactures continuously without seasonal inventory resets
questions.explain: The 7-season model (vs. traditional 4-season) allows Zara to commit only 15% of internal manufacturing early and reserve 85% for later stages when demand is clearer. External manufacturing splits 55% early / 45% late. This is operationalized 'responsiveness' — a structural choice that prioritizes flexibility over forecast accuracy.

questions.q: In supply chain strategy frameworks, what defines the 'push-pull boundary' or decoupling point?
options: The transition point where the supply chain shifts from forecast-driven to order-driven production
The location where finished goods are shipped to retail stores
The moment when customer payment is processed
The warehouse where inventory safety stock is held
The supplier where raw materials are purchased
questions.explain: The push-pull boundary marks where the supply chain strategy transitions: upstream is forecast-driven ('push'), downstream is order-driven ('pull'). For Zara, this boundary is quite far downstream due to postponement and fast information flow. Traditional fashion pushes far upstream; Zara pulls much closer to the customer.

questions.q: Which supply chain strategy is most appropriate for products with high SKU variety, high margins, and unpredictable demand?
options: Build-to-Order — fast fulfillment only after order is received
Make-to-Stock with finished goods inventory buffers
Push with work-in-progress (WIP) buffers for rapid fulfillment
JIT-Push — scheduled component delivery with final assembly flexibility
Kanban pull system with fixed reorder points
questions.explain: Build-to-Order minimizes inventory risk for high-margin, unpredictable products by producing only after demand is confirmed. Margins are high enough to justify longer lead times. 'JIT-Push' is a common trap because it sounds like it combines JIT with pull; in reality, it refers to component scheduling (push) while final assembly may vary.

questions.q: What is the defining characteristic of 7-Eleven's supply chain design philosophy?
options: Optimize for convenience, freshness, and item-by-item responsiveness using dense store clustering and tight IT integration
Minimize labor costs through automation and centralized distribution
Maximize inventory turns by carrying only the slowest-moving SKUs
Standardize product assortment across all store locations globally
Reduce supplier complexity by consolidating to a few large vendors
questions.explain: 7-Eleven's strategy revolves around convenience (便利性), freshness (新鮮度), and dense clustering (集群擴張) supported by tight IT-logistics integration. Item-by-item management (Tanpin Kanri) drives localized assortment decisions. This is fundamentally different from Zara's 'trend-chasing' approach — 7-Eleven optimizes for local demand precision and freshness.

questions.q: Why does 7-Eleven use joint delivery arrangements with multiple suppliers?
options: To consolidate deliveries and reduce truck trips, lowering logistics costs while improving frequency
To reduce negotiating power of individual suppliers
To ensure price competition among carriers
To comply with antitrust regulations
To simplify store-level inventory management software
questions.explain: Joint delivery pools shipments from multiple suppliers into shared delivery runs, achieving economies of scale (full truckloads) while increasing delivery frequency. This enables responsiveness: stores can order smaller quantities more often. It's a logistics design choice that supports the underlying 'freshness and frequency' strategy.

questions.q: According to 7-Eleven's product-demand classification, which type of product should use an efficient supply chain?
options: Functional products with stable, predictable demand
Products with innovative features and unpredictable demand
Products requiring customization and personalization
Newly launched items with uncertain market reception
Premium products with high margins and niche appeal
questions.explain: Functional products (basic staples with predictable demand) pair with efficient supply chains (cost-optimized, high-volume). Innovative products (new items, unpredictable demand) require responsive supply chains (fast, flexible). This classification allows 7-Eleven to route different products through different supply chain architectures.

questions.q: In 7-Eleven's hub-and-spoke distribution model, what type of ordering mechanism is used between stores and distributors?
options: Order-point (Kanban) pull based on actual shelf depletion
Supplier-initiated replenishment based on forecasts
Centralized push from distribution centers on fixed schedules
Demand-driven promotions triggered by seasonal events
Consensus-based ordering negotiated monthly
questions.explain: 7-Eleven uses OS/Pull/Kanban (看板) ordering: stores pull goods from distributors based on actual shelf needs, not forecasts. This reduces obsolescence and stockouts simultaneously. The hub-and-spoke structure (distributors as aggregation nodes) enables the economics to work: many small store orders consolidated into full truckloads upstream.

questions.q: What is the key distinction between speculative capacity and reactive capacity?
options: Speculative capacity is committed before accurate demand info exists; reactive capacity is committed after demand is 70-80% certain
Speculative capacity is cheaper; reactive capacity requires more skilled workers
Speculative capacity applies only to manufacturing; reactive capacity applies to distribution
Speculative capacity is used for high-volume products; reactive capacity for niche products
Speculative capacity reduces inventory; reactive capacity increases it
questions.explain: Speculative capacity (投機性產能) is committed under probabilistic forecasts before true demand arrives. Reactive capacity (反應性產能) is reserved and committed only after demand signals clarify. Both are deliberate supply chain design choices. The exam trap is thinking reactive is always superior — it typically incurs higher unit costs or requires premium lead-time agreements.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT a recommended tactic for building reactive capacity for quick response?
options: Increase forecast accuracy before committing capacity
Shorten production and material lead times
Accelerate information flow from customers to production
Reduce batch sizes to increase flexibility
Modify supply chain architecture to enable rapid production in response to actual demand
questions.explain: Reactive capacity for quick response focuses on structural changes (lead-time reduction, information acceleration, batch reduction, supply chain redesign). Improving forecast accuracy is a helpful but separate initiative; reactive capacity assumes that even with accurate forecasts, market dynamics demand flexibility. The four listed tactics all strengthen reactive capability; forecast improvement does not directly.

questions.q: When using reactive capacity to cope with inaccurate forecasts, which principle should guide the allocation of capacity between predictable and unpredictable items?
options: Reserve reactive capacity specifically for unpredictable items; produce low-risk predictable items early in the season
Allocate equal capacity to all items regardless of predictability
Produce all high-margin items early; postpone low-margin items
Use early-season sales data only for replenishing the top-performing 20% of SKUs
Implement strict inventory caps to force demand discipline
questions.explain: The strategy is to produce high-confidence, stable-demand items early using speculative capacity, then use the early-season demand (~20% of season) as a signal to allocate reactive capacity to high-risk items in later stages. This minimizes overstocking and lost sales simultaneously. The key insight is selective capacity commitment, not uniform production.

questions.q: What is the purpose of tracking customer segments, product attributes, and timing of demand in Tanpin Kanri?
options: To maximize revenue per shelf space by matching assortment and replenishment to granular local demand patterns
To reduce labor costs through workforce standardization
To eliminate the need for promotional pricing strategies
To centralize inventory planning across all 7-Eleven locations globally
To simplify vendor negotiation and payment terms
questions.explain: Tanpin Kanri (單品管理) — item-by-item management — focuses on optimizing each SKU individually. By understanding which customer segments buy what, when (time of day, day of week, season), and why, 7-Eleven can maximize revenue per limited shelf space. This is fundamentally a demand management philosophy, not a cost-cutting tool.

questions.q: How does 7-Eleven use weather data in its IT system to improve supply chain decisions?
options: To forecast product demand patterns — hot days drive iced beverage sales, rainy days affect store foot traffic and assortment needs
To adjust delivery frequencies based on seasonal demand peaks
To optimize warehouse storage temperature controls
To determine supplier payment terms based on seasonal volatility
To set variable pricing for weather-sensitive products
questions.explain: Weather data was added to 7-Eleven's IT system in the 1990s alongside POS and inventory data. The implicit use case is demand pattern recognition: external factors like temperature, humidity, and precipitation correlate with which products sell. This data supports Tanpin Kanri decision-making — if rain is forecast, assortment and replenishment decisions shift.

questions.q: What is the primary pitfall of ordering inventory based solely on POS (point-of-sale) data?
options: POS data records only completed sales — it cannot capture lost sales from stockouts, leading to systematic under-ordering
POS data ignores supplier lead times and safety stock requirements
POS data is too slow to update in real time for fast-moving items
POS data includes returns and discounts that inflate demand signals
POS data does not account for promotional price elasticity
questions.explain: If an item is out of stock during the measurement period, POS shows zero sales — but actual demand may have been much higher (lost sales, frustrated customers). Ordering from historical POS data therefore systematically under-orders high-demand items, especially those that experienced stockouts. This is an exam trap because POS seems like 'real' data, but it is truncated by supply constraints.

questions.q: Which factor explains why POS data patterns differ from one store to another in 7-Eleven?
options: Existing inventory levels — stockouts make sales appear lower than actual demand
Changes in assortment decisions — different product mix yields different sales patterns
Demand changes over time — historical POS may be irrelevant if trends shift
Discounts and weather — external factors distort recorded sales
Store manager training quality and education background
questions.explain: If inventory is depleted, POS records zero sales even though demand may have been strong. This inventory-demand mismatch is a core POS data pitfall. While other factors (assortment, trends, weather) also explain variations, the inventory effect is especially insidious because it creates negative feedback: low POS signals lead to lower replenishment, perpetuating understocking.

questions.q: Zara's markdown rate (50% of items marked down) compared to the industry average (15%) reflects which strategic choice?
options: Acceptance of rapid trend turnover and product obsolescence — high markdown is a cost of speed
Poor forecast accuracy and excess inventory
Oversized initial markups to absorb discounts later
Supplier quality issues requiring clearance sales
Market positioning as a discount or outlet brand
questions.explain: High markdown rates are a deliberate trade-off: Zara accepts rapid fashion obsolescence in exchange for trend-chasing speed. Small batches mean misses are limited in downside (only 10% fail vs. 1% industry average), but winners hit hard when identified and replenished. The markdown cost is budgeted as part of the responsiveness strategy, not a sign of failure.

questions.q: How does store-level decentralization of product mix decisions in Zara support its overall competitive strategy?
options: Allows local store managers to tailor assortment to customer demographics and local trends, improving relevance and responsiveness
Reduces headquarters coordination costs and bureaucratic delays
Eliminates the need for centralized demand forecasting
Simplifies supply chain planning by standardizing inventory across regions
Shifts inventory risk entirely to store managers
questions.explain: Store managers, not headquarters, decide the product mix for their location. This distributed decision-making keeps responsiveness high: a manager in Barcelona can note emerging local trends and request adjustments within the bi-weekly delivery cycle. It complements the closed-loop information system (Customer → Manager → Buyer → Designer) by empowering frontline intelligence.

questions.q: What is the relationship between Zara's batch size strategy and customer visit frequency?
options: Smaller batches create artificial scarcity, driving the 'buy now or never' psychology that generates 17 visits/year vs. 3 industry average
Smaller batches reduce inventory costs, lowering prices and attracting frequent shoppers
Smaller batches allow Zara to test designs and gradually scale successful ones
Smaller batches reduce markdown rates by matching supply more closely to demand
Smaller batches simplify warehouse operations and distribution logistics
questions.explain: Small batches deliberately create scarcity-driven urgency. Zara customers visit ~17 times/year (vs. ~3 industry average) because they fear missing out on limited items. This is a psychological lever, not a side effect. The strategy trades off operational efficiency (smaller batches = lower inventory) against psychological impact (scarcity creates urgency).

questions.q: In Zara's manufacturing strategy, what is the economic rationale for outsourcing 34% to Asia and 14% to Europe for basic items, while keeping 49% (high-fashion) in proximity (Spain/Portugal/Morocco)?
options: Asia and Europe handle commodity items with stable demand; proximity manufacturing handles fashion items requiring speed
Proximity manufacturing guarantees higher quality for premium items
Asia and Europe suppliers are contracted with long lead-time agreements
Proximity manufacturing reduces total inventory costs globally
Asia and Europe have stricter labor regulations for commodity production
questions.explain: Basic items (socks, basics) have stable demand and long lead times — they can be made in low-cost Asia months in advance. High-fashion items are unpredictable and require fast turnarounds (design feedback to store in 1 week) — they're made in proximity despite higher labor costs. The manufacturing footprint is optimized by product risk profile and lead-time requirements, not cost alone.

questions.q: How does 7-Eleven's temperature-controlled distribution strategy support its supply chain responsiveness?
options: It enables separate, frequent delivery runs for frozen, chilled, ambient, and hot items — improving freshness and reducing forced assortment compromises
It reduces spoilage and extends shelf life, lowering inventory costs
It eliminates the need for centralized warehousing
It allows stores to pre-stage inventory for predictable seasonal demand
It consolidates multiple supplier deliveries into single trucks
questions.explain: Separate temperature zones require separate delivery runs. This increases frequency (not decreases it) and enables precise fresh-item management per zone. A store can order just-in-time for hot foods (which spoil quickest) while ordering frozen items on longer cycles. It's a trade-off: higher logistics complexity for higher freshness and better demand matching.

questions.q: Which supply chain metric best captures the outcome of Zara's design-to-store operations in the 7-season model?
options: Design-to-store lead time as fast as 1 week — enabling rapid response to trend signals
Inventory turnover ratio — how many times inventory is sold and replaced
Markdown rate — percentage of items discounted
Order-fill rate — percentage of orders fulfilled on-time
Safety stock levels — inventory held to prevent stockouts
questions.explain: The 7-season model's strategic outcome is a 1-week design-to-store lead time, which directly enables Zara's trend-chasing model. This speed is the operationalized responsiveness. While turnover and markdown are influenced by the model, they are not the primary success metric. Lead time is the capability that enables all downstream competitive advantages.

questions.q: What does the closed-loop information flow (Customer → Store Manager → Buyer → Designer → Production Manager) reveal about Zara's organizational structure?
options: It integrates front-line customer intelligence directly into design and production planning — flattening traditional functional silos
It is highly centralized, with all decisions made by the production manager
It segregates sales from operations to maintain quality control
It requires multiple levels of approval, slowing decision-making
It relies on consumer research panels rather than in-store observation
questions.explain: The information loop is intentionally short and direct: customer feedback reaches the designer and production manager in hours, not months. This is a structural choice that tightens the feedback loop, reducing latency. It contrasts sharply with traditional fashion (where designers work in isolation). The structure embodies the responsiveness philosophy.

questions.q: If 7-Eleven observes that early-season sales (20% of the selling period) for an unpredictable item are strong, which capacity allocation decision should it make?
options: Reserve reactive capacity to fulfill additional demand in the remaining 80% of the season
Increase speculative capacity for that item in the remaining 80% of the season
Reduce all further production to avoid overstocking
Immediately mark down prices to liquidate early inventory
Switch to a competitor's equivalent product
questions.explain: Strong early-season sales (20% signal) suggest robust actual demand. The correct response is to allocate reactive capacity (reserved for high-risk items) to fulfill the remaining 80% of the season based on this clarified demand signal. This minimizes the risk of both overstocking (if demand drops) and stockouts (if it continues strong).

questions.q: Why does 7-Eleven cluster store growth densely in one region before expanding to the next, rather than spreading stores thinly across many regions?
options: To achieve economies of scale in shared distribution infrastructure — a dense cluster supports efficient hub-and-spoke logistics and justifies IT investment
To build brand awareness through advertising saturation
To reduce competition from rival convenience store chains
To simplify financial reporting across geographic units
To comply with real estate zoning regulations
questions.explain: Clustered growth (集群成長) enables 7-Eleven to share distribution centers, temperature-controlled vehicles, and IT systems across many nearby stores. A thinly spread network would require multiple, inefficient distribution nodes. Clustering is a structural choice that makes the responsive, multi-temperature delivery model economically viable.

lectures.title: Lecture 11: Revenue Management and Pricing Decisions: Supply Chain Issues, and Multi-Product Cases

questions.q: In the double marginalization model with c = $7, what is the supplier's optimal wholesale price (w*)?
questions.explain: The supplier maximizes π_s = (w − 7)q*, where q* = 50 − w. Solving the first-order condition yields w* = $28.5. Students often confuse this with marginal cost ($7) or guess a linear interpolation; the correct answer comes from profit-maximization, not cost recovery.

questions.q: Under double marginalization with w* = $28.5 and c = $7, what is the resulting retail price?
questions.explain: The retailer's first-order condition is p* = 25 + w/2 = 25 + 14.25 = $39.25. A common trap is choosing the supply-chain-optimal price ($28.5) or the wholesale price itself; both are incorrect because the retailer still adds its margin.

questions.q: What is the total channel profit under decentralized double marginalization versus the integrated supply-chain optimum?
questions.explain: Decentralized profit is $693.38; integrated optimum is $924.50. The efficiency loss of ~25% occurs because each party externalizes costs onto the other. Reversing these numbers or using the individual retailer/supplier profits leads to the wrong answer.

questions.q: Why does double marginalization result in both the wholesale AND retail price being above supply-chain-optimal levels?
options: Only the retailer adds margin; the supplier has no choice
The supplier ignores the retailer's lost revenue; the retailer ignores the supplier's foregone profit
Retail competition forces prices up equally
The demand curve is inelastic at high prices
Consumer willingness to pay increases with price
questions.explain: The root cause is externality: each party ignores the cost it imposes on the other. The supplier raises w without considering the retailer's response (lower demand); the retailer raises p without considering the supplier's margin squeeze. This exam trap catches students who think only the retailer is at fault.

questions.q: Under the two-part tariff remedial mechanism, how does the supplier recover profit if the wholesale price is set to marginal cost?
options: Through per-unit price increases over time
Via a fixed franchise fee charged to the retailer
By exclusive dealing restrictions
Through quantity rebates
By controlling the retail price directly
questions.explain: The two-part tariff combines w = c (zero per-unit margin) with a fixed fee F that the supplier charges upfront. This restores incentive compatibility because the retailer now maximizes as if vertically integrated. High demand uncertainty breaks this mechanism because the fixed fee may over/undercompensate.

questions.q: Which remedial measure for double marginalization is LEAST effective when demand uncertainty is high?
options: Vertical integration
Marginal cost pricing with franchise fee
Quantity discounts
Retail competition
Buy-backs
questions.explain: The two-part tariff (franchise fee) requires that the fixed fee can be calibrated to split value fairly. Under high demand uncertainty, the realized demand may be far from expected, making the fixed fee either too high or too low, breaking coordination. Buy-backs, by contrast, shift risk and work better under uncertainty.

questions.q: When two competing retailers source from the same supplier, how does retail competition affect the supply-chain profit versus the single-retailer case?
options: It decreases profit from $693.38 to $631.12
It increases profit from $693.38 to $821.78
It has no effect on wholesale or retail prices
It decreases profit to supply-chain optimal $924.50
It increases profit to $950.00
questions.explain: With competing retailers, the supply-chain profit rises to $821.78 from the single-retailer level of $693.38 (still below integrated optimum of $924.50). Competition erodes each retailer's margin but increases total volume and channel efficiency. Students often assume competition raises prices or has no effect.

questions.q: What is the primary insight of the seven core concepts of revenue management regarding cost versus price?
options: Cost-based pricing always maximizes profit
Focus on price rather than costs when balancing supply and demand
Marginal cost should equal marginal revenue at all times
Costs should be transparent to customers
Price and cost always move together
questions.explain: Concept #1 explicitly states: replace cost-based pricing with market-based pricing (i.e., focus on price).
