This shift is fundamental to revenue management — the value customers will pay, not the cost to serve, drives profit. Students who focus on cost containment miss the primary lever.

questions.q: In the single-price demand example with a linear curve from 1 to 99 units, what is the profit-maximizing price and quantity?
options: $25 per unit, 75 units sold
$50 per unit, 50 units sold
$60 per unit, 40 units sold
$75 per unit, 25 units sold
$100 per unit, 1 unit sold
questions.explain: At the single price of $50, the firm sells 50 units for revenue of $2,500. This is the profit-maximizing point on a linear demand curve. Segmented pricing (four tiers: $20, $40, $60, $80) sells 80 units, yielding ~60% higher revenue despite lower per-unit prices.

questions.q: What does consumer surplus represent in the context of single-price versus segmented pricing?
options: The total profit the firm earns from a customer
Willingness to Pay minus the Offered Price
The cost of producing the good
The difference between retail and wholesale prices
The total revenue divided by units sold
questions.explain: Consumer surplus = WTP − Offered Price. Under a single price, high-valuation customers earn surplus; low-valuation customers are rejected. Segmented pricing reduces both forms of waste by capturing more of the willingness to pay. This is a definition question; students often confuse it with firm profit.

questions.q: When segmented pricing is introduced with four price tiers ($20, $40, $60, $80), how many units are sold compared to the single $50 price?
options: 50 units (no change)
65 units (~30% increase)
80 units (~60% increase)
100 units (all units)
40 units (fewer than single price)
questions.explain: Segmented pricing sells ~80 units versus 50 under the single average price — a 60% increase in volume and revenue. This refers to revenue/volume, not profit margin. Students sometimes confuse this with a 60% profit margin improvement, which is an exam trap.

questions.q: In the booking limit problem, when capacity is insufficient for both customer segments, what does the critical ratio determine?
options: The percentage of seats to oversell
How many seats to reserve for the high-value customer type
The maximum price the firm can charge
The break-even point for demand
The daily occupancy rate needed
questions.explain: The critical ratio CR = (p_H − p_L) / p_H tells the firm how many seats to reserve for H-type customers. A higher ratio means higher underage cost (lost H revenue) relative to overage cost (lost L revenue), justifying more reserves. This follows the newsvendor framework.

questions.q: What is the overage cost in the booking limit framework?
options: Zero — an empty seat has no cost
The marginal cost of operating the flight
The revenue lost from not selling an L-type seat
The price paid by the H-type customer
The difference between H and L prices
questions.explain: The overage cost = p_L (revenue foregone by not selling the L-type seat if you reserve it for H). A common exam trap is setting overage cost to zero, which artificially inflates the optimal reserve quantity. The cost is opportunity cost, not actual outlay.

questions.q: When the critical ratio equals 0.5 (equal underage and overage costs), what is the optimal booking limit for L-type customers?
options: Reserve mean H demand, no z-score adjustment
Reserve mean H demand plus one standard deviation
Reserve half of total capacity
Reserve based on L-type demand distribution only
Reserve all remaining capacity after H demand
questions.explain: When CR = 0.5, underage cost = overage cost, so you reserve exactly the mean H demand (NORMSINV(0.5) = 0, yielding mean + 0×stdev). The L-type booking limit is then Capacity − Mean H demand. No z-adjustment is needed because costs are symmetric.

questions.q: In the booking limit example with p_H = $1,500, p_L = $500, what is the critical ratio and how does it affect the reserve quantity?
options: 0.50; reserve mean H demand
0.67; reserve mean H demand plus NORMSINV(0.67)×stdev
0.75; reserve 75% of capacity
1.00; reserve all capacity for H
0.33; reserve one-third of capacity
questions.explain: CR = ($1,500 − $500) / $1,500 = $1,000 / $1,500 ≈ 0.667 ≈ 2/3. Because underage cost ($1,000) exceeds overage cost ($500), you reserve more than mean — specifically, mean + NORMSINV(2/3)×stdev of H demand. This reflects higher penalty for turning away H customers.

questions.q: Which of the following is NOT listed as a remedial mechanism for double marginalization in the lecture notes?
options: Vertical integration
Marginal cost pricing
Quantity discounts
Aggressive advertising to increase demand
Buy-backs
questions.explain: The lecture lists seven mechanisms: vertical integration, marginal cost pricing, franchise fee/two-part tariff, quantity discounts, supplier-set retail price, competition, and buy-backs. Aggressive advertising is not among them. This recall question tests student attention to the specific list.

questions.q: How does dynamic pricing differ from willingness-to-pay pricing in the revenue management toolkit?
options: Dynamic pricing uses real-time inventory/demand; WTP pricing is a static segment-based approach
Willingness-to-pay pricing adjusts inventory; dynamic pricing is cost-based
Both are identical terms used interchangeably
Dynamic pricing only applies to airlines; WTP applies to software
Dynamic pricing ignores demand; WTP ignores price
questions.explain: Dynamic pricing adjusts price continuously in response to inventory levels and evolving demand (real-time). Willingness-to-pay pricing estimates each segment's maximum price and sets price accordingly (static per segment). They are distinct mechanisms for capturing value.

questions.q: What is overbooking in the context of revenue management?
options: Charging customers multiple times for one booking
Intentionally selling more than capacity to hedge against cancellations
Reserving capacity without allowing bookings
Forcing customers to upgrade to higher-priced products
Reducing prices to fill unused capacity
questions.explain: Overbooking is deliberate overselling to compensate for expected cancellations. Airlines use this to maintain high load factors. Students sometimes confuse it with fraud or capacity constraints; it is a legitimate revenue management tactic.

questions.q: In multi-product revenue management, what is the primary challenge when multiple customer segments can freely self-select across products?
options: All segments will choose the cheapest product
Incentive compatibility requires adjusting prices downward on higher-tier products to prevent profitable defection
The firm has no way to segment customers
Segments will always choose the product with highest development cost
Demand uncertainty makes pricing impossible
questions.explain: If you price Advanced at each segment's full valuation ($2,500 for Large Corps), they may defect to a lower-priced product if it yields positive surplus. Incentive-compatible pricing requires strategic price cuts on premium products. This exam trap catches students who simply charge maximum WTP per segment.

questions.q: According to the lecture, how many customer segments are involved in the multi-product revenue management case study?
options: 2 segments
3 segments
4 segments
5 segments
6 segments
questions.explain: The case lists five segments: Consultants, Small Businesses, Large Corporations, Laboratories, and Students. Each has different willingness to pay and market size. This is a straightforward recall question testing student attention to the multi-product setup.

questions.q: In the multi-product case, what special pricing consideration applies to the Students segment?
options: They have the highest willingness to pay
They must pay at least marginal cost
They face a 40% bookstore commission, reducing net price received
They are price-insensitive
They can purchase unlimited quantities
questions.explain: The Students segment notes '40% bookstore commission,' meaning the firm receives only 60% of the selling price. This impacts profitability calculations and optimal pricing. Students often overlook this detail when analyzing multi-product strategies.

questions.q: What is the development cost for the Advanced product in the multi-product case?
questions.explain: The Advanced product has a $500,000 development cost with $35/unit marginal cost. Basic is $100,000; Professional is $200,000. This determines the minimum revenue needed to justify each product's existence. Students should memorize these fixed costs when evaluating profitability.

questions.q: For the Large Corporations segment, which product do they value most highly in the multi-product case?
options: Basic ($150 valuation)
Professional ($1,200 valuation)
Advanced ($2,500 valuation)
All products equally
None — large corporations prefer wholesale pricing
questions.explain: Large Corporations value Advanced at $2,500, Professional at $1,200, and Basic at $150. Their high valuation for premium products suggests these customers would benefit most from advanced features. This ranking is critical to understanding segment behavior.

questions.q: Which market segment has the largest potential market size in the multi-product case?
options: Consultants (20,000 units)
Small Businesses (15,000 units)
Large Corporations (5,000 units)
Laboratories (2,000 units)
Students (500,000 units)
questions.explain: Students represent 500,000 units of potential demand, dwarfing all other segments. However, their low valuation ($50–$100 per product) and bookstore commission make per-unit profit margins very thin. Volume versus margin is the trade-off here.

questions.q: In the context of second-degree price discrimination (two-tier pricing), what does 'second-degree' refer to?
options: Pricing that is illegal in most jurisdictions
Pricing by quantity or product version, not by customer identity
Pricing that only affects the second-largest segment
Pricing that charges different rates in different time periods
Pricing that is lower than first-degree discrimination
questions.explain: Second-degree price discrimination separates customers through product design or quantity (e.g., Basic vs. Professional), allowing customers to self-select. First-degree (perfect discrimination) charges each customer their max WTP; third-degree charges different groups different prices by identity. This is a conceptual question on discrimination types.

questions.q: What condition must be satisfied for the two-part tariff to effectively coordinate the supply chain?
options: Demand must be perfectly elastic
Demand uncertainty must be low
Demand must be perfectly inelastic
The supplier must own the retailer
The retailer must have perfect foresight
questions.explain: The two-part tariff works when demand uncertainty is LOW because the fixed fee F is calibrated to split expected profits. High uncertainty means actual demand diverges significantly from the forecast, rendering F either too high or too low, breaking coordination. This is a direct consequence of the fee structure.

questions.q: When comparing single-price to segmented pricing on a linear demand curve from 1 to 99 units, what is the main source of the ~60% revenue increase?
options: Higher profit margins on each unit sold
Serving rejected low-valuation customers and capturing high-valuation surplus
Reduction in production costs due to scale
Elimination of competition
Regulatory approval for premium pricing
questions.explain: Segmented pricing increases revenue by selling to both high-valuation customers (who were earning surplus under single price) and previously rejected low-valuation customers. At $50, only 50 units sell; at tiered prices, 80 units sell. The increase is in volume and value capture, not margin per unit.

questions.q: How many business development cost categories are included in the multi-product case?
options: One cost category for all segments
One per product (Basic, Professional, Advanced)
One per segment (Consultants, Small Businesses, etc.)
One for fixed costs and one for variable costs
One for each combination of product and segment
questions.explain: The case lists a business development cost for each of the five segments (Consultants $200k, Small Businesses $200k, Large Corporations $150k, Laboratories $100k, Students $300k), reflecting the effort to penetrate each market. These are separate from product development costs.

questions.q: In the booking limit problem, how is the underage cost defined?
options: The revenue lost from selling a seat to L-type instead of H-type
The cost of operating an empty seat
The cost of bumping a customer
The difference between fixed and variable costs
The tax on unsold inventory
questions.explain: Underage cost = p_H − p_L, the foregone revenue from not reserving enough seats for H-type customers (turning them away when you later sell to L-type). This contrasts with overage cost (p_L, opportunity cost of unsold seat). Students sometimes reverse these or set underage to zero.

questions.q: What does the newsvendor framework in the booking limit context represent?
options: A supply chain decision where a news vendor sets wholesale prices
A problem where overstock (overage) and understock (underage) costs must be balanced
A model that applies only to retail inventory management
A demand forecasting method used exclusively for newspapers
A fixed price decision with no dynamic adjustment
questions.explain: The newsvendor model mirrors the booking limit problem: reserve too many seats for H → overage cost (unsold capacity); reserve too few → underage cost (turned-away H customers). The critical ratio balances these two costs. This is the conceptual foundation of the booking limit approach.
